coredata.dc:title: Binder-free supercapacitor electrode with composite of graphene quantum dots and gold nanoparticles
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Sharifi, Samira
Dejpasand, Mohamad Taghi
Rahimi, Kourosh
Yazdani, Ahmad
Saievar-Iranizad, Esmaiel
coredata.dc:description: We fabricated binder-free energy storage supercapacitor using two-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and a composite of GQDs and gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) through facile hydrothermal and photochemical methods. The structures were analyzed using X-ray diffractometry and Raman spectroscopy, while transmission electron microscopy was used to observe their morphologies. Cyclic voltammetry, galvanostatic charging/discharging, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy were used to investigate the electrochemical properties. Our findings suggest that the GQDs/Au nanocomposite demonstrates a higher specific capacitance (480 F/g) in comparison to GQDs (203 F/g). The nanocomposites exhibited a specific power of 106 W/kg and a specific energy of 10.6 Wh/kg at a current density of 1 A/g. Moreover, the nanocomposite displayed reasonable charge/discharge stability after 2000 charge-discharge cycles with a capacitance retention of ~79 %. Besides, a density functional theory study showed that an internal electric field is formed across the interface of Au and graphene, which is beneficial for charge storage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical energy storage
Supercapacitor
Graphene quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles
Hydrothermal
Photochemical

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of GQDs and GQDs/Au composite

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schemes of the preparation of the GQDs and GQDs/Au composite coated on Ni foam substrates.
(A) The XRD pattern and (B) the Raman spectrum of the GQDs/Au nanocomposite.
TEM micrograph of (A) GQDs and (B) the GQDs/Au nanocomposite.
(A) Absorbance spectra of the GQDs and GQDs/Au samples. Tauc plots of the (B) GQDs and (C) GQDs/Au samples.

CV curves of (A) GQDs and (B) GQDs/Au electrodes at different scan rates. GCD curves of the (C) GQDs and (D) GQDs/Au electrodes for different current densities. (E) Comparative (GQDs versus GQDs/Au electrodes) CV curves at the scan rate of 5 mV/s and (F) GCD curves at the current density of 1 A/g.
Comparing electrochemical behavior of the GQDs and GQDs/Au electrodes: (A) EIS curves, (B) Ragone plot, and (C) charge-discharge cycling ability for 2000 cycles at a current density of 20 A/g.

Electrochemical performance of the (GQDs/Au)//AC asymmetric setup: (A) CV curves at the scan rate of 20 mV/s within various potential windows. (B) CV curves within the potential window of 0.0–1.5 V for different scan rates. (C) GCD curves within the potential window of 0.0–1.5 V for different current densities. (D) The asymmetric assembly powering a red LED.
The planar averaged electrostatic potential along the perpendicular axis of the interface of Au(111)/Graphene.
Specific capacitance, energy, and power of the samples at the current density of 1 A/g.

head.ce:title: Binder-free supercapacitor electrode with composite of graphene quantum dots and gold nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Condensed Matter Physics Group, Faculty of Basic Science, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Condensed Matter Physics Group, Faculty of Basic Science, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Condensed Matter Physics, Faculty of Physics, Alzahra University, Tehran, 19938-93973, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Condensed Matter Physics, Faculty of Physics, Alzahra University, Tehran, 19938-93973, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We fabricated binder-free energy storage supercapacitor using two-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and a composite of GQDs and gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) through facile hydrothermal and photochemical methods. The structures were analyzed using X-ray diffractometry and Raman spectroscopy, while transmission electron microscopy was used to observe their morphologies. Cyclic voltammetry, galvanostatic charging/discharging, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy were used to investigate the electrochemical properties. Our findings suggest that the GQDs/Au nanocomposite demonstrates a higher specific capacitance (480 F/g) in comparison to GQDs (203 F/g). The nanocomposites exhibited a specific power of 106 W/kg and a specific energy of 10.6 Wh/kg at a current density of 1 A/g. Moreover, the nanocomposite displayed reasonable charge/discharge stability after 2000 charge-discharge cycles with a capacitance retention of ~79 %. Besides, a density functional theory study showed that an internal electric field is formed across the interface of Au and graphene, which is beneficial for charge storage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors, which are also known as ultracapacitors, have emerged as up-and-coming energy storage devices owing to their distinctive blend of high power density, fast charge-discharge rates, and longer cycle life. These devices bridge the existing gap between traditional batteries and capacitors, offering an efficient and versatile solution for various energy storage applications [1–4]. The functioning of supercapacitors is based on the concept of storing electrostatic charge at the interface of the electrode and electrolyte. Unlike traditional capacitors that rely on energy storage through charge separation in dielectric materials, supercapacitors employ electrochemical processes involving ion adsorption or redox reactions occurring at the interface of electrode and electrolyte [5]. The mechanism allows them to achieve higher energy densities than traditional capacitors while retaining fast charge-discharge capabilities. In the charging process, ions from the electrolyte are attracted and adsorbed over the electrode surface, leading to the formation of an electrical double-layer [6]. This electrical double-layer comprises the Helmholtz layer, where ions are directly adsorbed onto the electrode surface, and the diffuse layer, where ions are distributed in the electrolyte near the electrode. This process involves reversible and fast ion adsorption/desorption, enabling supercapacitors to exhibit high power densities.

In addition to ion adsorption-based supercapacitors, some pseudocapacitors utilize redox (reduction and oxidation) reactions at the interface of electrode and electrolyte to store and release energy. Materials with pseudocapacitance behavior, such as conducting polymers and transition metal oxides/hydroxides, undergo faradaic reactions involving electron transfer and ion adsorption/desorption processes [7,8]. This redox behavior results in higher capacitance compared to purely electrical double-layer capacitance, offering higher energy densities [9,10].

Carbon-based materials have recently gained significant interest due to their unique optical and electrochemical properties originating from their confined size [11,12]. Extensive research has been conducted on the use of carbon-based materials, including activated carbon (AC), carbon nanotubes (CNT), and graphene (G), for supercapacitor applications [13–15]. The combination of high surface area, good electrical conductivity, chemical stability, and flexibility in these materials is beneficial for maximizing capacitance and minimizing resistance in supercapacitor electrodes [16–18]. Among them, graphene-based materials including graphene, graphene oxide (GO), and graphene quantum dot (GQD) offer advantages such as low-cost synthesis, tunable surface functionalization, and compatibility with various electrode architectures [19–25].

In 2008, Stoller et al. [26] fabricated an electrochemical double-layer supercapacitor based on chemically modified graphene and found that while GO sheets could disperse evenly in water, they are agglomerated during the reduction process. Despite this, the graphene electrode demonstrated a specific capacitance of 135 F/g in KOH electrolyte attributed to the notably large specific surface area of graphene. In a work by Wang et al. [27] in 2009, the graphene sheets were prepared via a hydrazine reduction method to prevent excessive stacking of graphene oxide (GO) sheets, and a maximum specific capacitance of 205 F/g was obtained in an aqueous electrolyte. Zhang et al. [28] researchers synthesized GQDs of uniform size, and the electrode based on these GQDs demonstrated impressive characteristics, including a high specific capacitance of 296.7 F/g, excellent specific energy of 41.2 Wh/kg at 1 A/g, low internal resistance, short relaxation time, and exceptional cycling stability.

Additionally, incorporating metals into carbon-based materials has shown potential for further enhancing their electrochemical performance. Metals such as gold, silver, and nickel can improve the conductivity and stability of carbon-based materials, leading to enhanced charge storage capacity and prolonged cycling life [29,30]. The synergy between carbon dots and metal composites holds the potential for further improving the overall performance of supercapacitors, combining the unique properties of carbon dots with the advantages of metal incorporation.

Shabani et al. [31] synthesized a hybrid supercapacitor based on polyaniline/reduced graphene oxide/gold nanoparticles and found a higher specific capacitance, power, and energy as compared to the bare polyaniline supercapacitor (303 vs. 190 F/g at the current density of 1 mA/cm2). Sahoo et al. [32] prepared a 3D graphene aerogel integrated with gold nanoparticles as a high-performance supercapacitor. Their electrode exhibited a specific capacitance of 554 F/g, a specific energy of 10.7 Wh/kg, and a specific power of 203.5 W/kg with a capacitance retention stability of ~91 %. Chen et al. [33] developed a flexible supercapacitor electrode by depositing a uniform ultrathin 7-nm gold film onto monolayer graphene. Their work exhibited a capacitance of 81.5 μF/cm2 at a scan rate of 0.1 V/s, which is significantly higher (~17×) compared to the device utilizing only monolayer graphene. Xia et al. [34] have formed a GQD/Co(OH)2 composite on nanoporous Au-Ag wires, studying GQD effects on the supercapacitance performance. The composite showed improved specific capacity (273 vs. 151 mC/cm2).

In general, the binder-free preparation method in coating of active materials on Ni foam substrate offer advantages including reduced internal resistance, improved energy density, and flexibility. Regarding the internal resistance, since traditional supercapacitor electrodes consist of a mixture of active material (such as activated carbon) and a binder (such as PVDF), the binder helps maintain the structural integrity of the electrode, but it also introduces additional internal resistance due to its insulating nature. Binder-free electrodes eliminate this extra resistance by directly depositing the active material onto the current collector without using a binder. Regarding the energy density, since in binder-free approaches, the active material adheres directly to the conducting substrate (such as Ni foam) without the need for a binder, which reduces the inactive mass associated with binders, leading to a higher energy density. Regarding the flexibility, since binders add rigidity to the electrode, binder-free electrodes are more flexible and can conform to irregular shapes, making them suitable for wearable and flexible devices.

Here, we compared the electrochemical energy storage by graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and a composite of GQDs and Au nanoparticles. First, we characterized their structural and morphological properties and then their supercapacitance using both three-electrode and asymmetric two-electrode setups using cyclic voltammetry, galvanostatic charging/discharging, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy techniques.

The precursors used in the research were employed in their original form with no purification. Glucose (≥99.5 %) and HAuCl4 (99 %) were prepared from Sigma Aldrich Co., and N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) (99 %), acetonitrile (99 %), polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF), and activated carbon (extra pure) were prepared from Merck Co.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs and GQDs/Au composite
ce:section.ce:para: Via a hydrothermal process, GQDs were synthesized. Firstly, 0.02 g of glucose was dissolved in 20 ml of deionized water, and then stirred for 15 min at room temperature. The resulting solution was placed into a stainless-steel autoclave and heated at a temperature of 180 °C for 6 h, resulting in a light-yellow solution. Subsequently, the solution was dialyzed using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 3.5 kDa to remove oversized particles. To synthesize the GQDs/Au nanocomposite, 2 ml of HAuCl4 aqueous solution (1.5 mM) was added to the 20 ml of synthesized GQDs dispersion (1 mg/ml). The obtained dispersion was then irradiated with a UVC lamp (16 W) for 2 h. This photochemical process results in the dissociation of HAuCl4 into Au3+, H+, and Cl− ions and the excitation of electrons in the GQDs by UV photons. Here two steps will take place: The first step is the formation of a bond between graphene quantum dots and gold, which occurs due to the higher reduction potential of gold compared to graphene quantum dots. The second step will be the assembly of the particles together and the formation of the GQDs/Au composite, which happens through aurophilic interaction (between Au atoms) and hydrogen bonds (between hydrogen from one graphene quantum dot and oxygen from another one). The pH level of the solution was maintained within 7–8 throughout the experiment. Finally, a light purple solution was obtained. The process of our synthesis procedure is demonstrated in Fig. 1 , outlining the various steps involved.

Various characterization techniques were employed to analyze the properties and features of the samples in this study. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was utilized to investigate the crystalline structure of the materials. XRD patterns were recorded using a Philips X'pert instrument with a Cu kα (0.154 nm) radiation source. By analyzing the diffraction patterns, valuable information about the crystal phases, lattice parameters, and crystallographic orientations of the samples can be obtained. Raman spectroscopy probes the materials' vibrational modes and molecular structure. Raman spectra were obtained using a Takram P50C0R10 Raman spectrometer (λex = 532 nm). This technique provides insights into the chemical bonding, presence of functional groups, and overall structural characteristics of the samples. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was utilized to visualize the materials' morphologies and microstructures. The samples were observed using a high-resolution CM30 300 kV TEM instrument. This technique directly imagines the sample's microstructure and provides valuable information about particle sizes, shapes, and distribution. The optical properties of the samples, specifically the bandgap energy, were examined using a Perkin-Elmer Lambda 25 spectrophotometer. This instrument measures the absorbance spectra of the materials across a range of wavelengths. The optical bandgap energy can be determined by analyzing the absorption characteristics, providing insights into the material's electronic structure and optical properties.

Using a three-electrode configuration submerged in a 3 M KOH aqueous solution, electrochemical measurements were conducted with the assistance of a Bio-Logic Science Instruments Multichannel Potentiostat/Galvanostat/EIS instrument that had VSP-300. The counter electrode consisted of a platinum electrode, the reference electrode was an Ag/AgCl electrode, and the working electrode used in the experiment was a square-shaped Ni foam substrate measuring 1 cm by 1 cm. This substrate underwent an in-situ coating process using materials that were previously prepared for the experiment. The GQDs and GQDs/Au solution were placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave to synthesize the working electrode. The Ni foam substrate was washed with deionized water, acetone, and ethanol before being placed inside the autoclave. After that, the autoclave was heated in an oven at a temperature of 180 °C for 6 h. Following this, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature in ambient air. The nickel foam coated with material was rinsed several times using deionized water and then dried at a temperature of 70 °C for 2 h. A two-electrode setup was prepared using a 3 M KOH aqueous electrolyte solution. In the asymmetric two-electrode supercapacitor device, the working electrode was the positive electrode, while activated carbon was used as the negative electrode. The anode electrode was prepared as described earlier. The cathode electrode (activated carbon) was prepared by mixing 95 wt% AC and 5 wt% PVDF in N-Methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) solvent. The resulting slurry was coated onto a nickel foam substrate using a brush with a mass loading of approximately 3 mg/cm2. The electrode was then dried in an oven at 60 °C for 10 h. The optimal mass ratio between the mass of the active material in positive and negative electrodes (m+/m−) was finely tuned according to
m +
m −
=
C S −
∆
V −
C s +
∆
V +
[35], where m, Cs, and ΔV are the mass of the active material, the specific capacitance, and the voltage range for positive (+) and negative (−) electrodes, respectively. A filter paper soaked in 3 M KOH electrolyte was used to separate the electrodes. These electrode configurations and preparation methods were employed to facilitate accurate electrochemical measurements and assess the performance of the synthesized materials in supercapacitor applications.

The XRD pattern of the GQDs/Au composite is shown in Fig. 2 (A). The XRD pattern displays a weak and broad peak centered around 23.5°, corresponding to the characteristic graphitic peak of the GQDs in the nanocomposite [36]. Additionally, two distinct peaks are observed at 42.9° and 49°, corresponding to the (111) and (200) crystallographic planes of the face-centered cubic (FCC) structure of Au nanospheres. The preservation of the crystallinity of both GQDs and Au nanospheres, as evident from the XRD pattern, indicates the formation of a unique morphology, which is critical for optimizing the electrical and optical properties of the composite sample.

Fig. 2 (B) presents the Raman shift spectrum of the GQDs/Au nanocomposite. The spectrum reveals two prominent peaks at 1435 cm−1 and 1526 cm−1. The peak observed at 1435 cm−1 signifies the presence of graphitic sp2 carbon domains associated with disordered (D) regions, while the peak at 1526 cm−1 corresponds to the crystalline (G) bands. In comparison, graphite exhibits Raman peaks at 1350 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, where the former relates to incomplete bonds and the latter corresponds to the well-ordered structure of graphite [37,38]. The observed shifts in the Raman peaks of the GQDs/Au nanocomposite can be attributed to multiple factors. First, the electron exchange between the GQDs and Au NPs influences the local electronic structure, resulting in peak displacements. Second, the layered structure and fragmentation of the graphene quantum dots, as opposed to the pristine graphite, contribute to further alterations in the Raman signatures. These observations indicate the unique characteristics of the GQDs/Au nanocomposite, highlighting its distinctive structural and electronic properties [37].

TEM micrographs of the synthesized GQDs and the GQDs/Au nanocomposite are depicted in Fig. 3 (A) and (B), respectively. From Fig. 3 (A), the size analysis of the GQDs reveals sizes in the range 2–5 nm, with an estimated average size of ~3 nm. The TEM image in Fig. 3 (B) showcases the GQDs/Au nanocomposite. The image reveals a larger particle size distribution, ranging from 15 to 50 nm. The average size of approximately 30 nm is determined by analyzing multiple particles in the image. These findings highlight the formation of the nanocomposite structure, where the Au nanoparticles are integrated with the GQDs. The larger particle size and the presence of Au nanoparticles increase the composite's size, potentially influencing its properties and performance.

UV–Vis absorption analysis was conducted to examine the optical characteristics of the samples that were synthesized. The absorption spectra and Tauc's plots of the colloidal solutions containing the bare GQDs and the GQDs/Au nanocomposite are depicted in Fig. 4 . In the absorption spectra, a peak at 241 nm is observed, which can be attributed to the sp2 hybridized structure resulting from CC and CC bonds within the GQDs [36]. This peak signifies π-electron transitions within the conjugated carbon network. Another peak at 295 nm is identified, which corresponds to the sp3 hybridized structure arising from CO bonds and is associated with the n-π* transition [39]. Upon adding Au nanospheres and the induction of surface plasmon resonance (SPR), a new peak emerges at 530 nm, as depicted in Fig. 4 (A). This peak signifies the SPR absorption phenomenon in the visible region, resulting from the interaction between the GQDs and Au nanospheres. The samples' optical bandgap (Eg) was calculated through the Tauc's relation [40]:
(1)
αh ν
n
= B
hν − Eg
where α represents the absorption coefficient, ν denotes the photon frequency, h stands for Planck's constant, B is a constant, n is a parameter based on the bandgap type (i.e. 1/2 for direct and 2 for indirect band gap types), and Eg signifies the optical bandgap. The Tauc's plots, presented in Fig. 4 (B) and (C), provide valuable information about the bandgaps of the synthesized samples. The optical bandgap of the bare GQDs is calculated to be 3.7 eV, while the GQDs/Au composite exhibits a smaller optical bandgap of 1.3 eV. This reduction in the bandgap of the composite is attributed to the formation of the SPR absorption peak in the visible region, resulting from the integration of Au nanospheres. The UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy analysis of the prepared samples provides insights into their optical characteristics, including specific peaks corresponding to different structural features and the modulation of bandgap energy by incorporating Au nanospheres.

The cyclic voltammetry (CV) technique is an electrochemical method widely used to find out the behavior of materials in solution. In this study, cyclic voltammetry was used to examine the electrochemical characteristics of the synthesized samples at room temperature. The CV curves, depicted in Fig. 5 (A, B), illustrate the current response of the electrodes as a function of the applied potential using different scan rates across a range of values from 5 to 50 mV/s. Following the analysis of the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves, clear redox peaks are evident in the voltage range of 0–0.5 V, signifying the presence of electrochemical reactions occurring at the electrode surface. These redox peaks correspond to the faradic current, which is associated with the transfer of electrons during electrochemical reactions. Fig. 5 (A) displays the CV curves of the GQDs electrode, where redox peaks are observed at 0.29, 0.32, 0.25, and 0.15 V. The observed peaks may be linked to the existence of carbon functional groups on the surface of the graphene quantum dots. The specific functional groups contribute to the electrochemical behavior of the GQDs and influence their performance as electrode materials [41]. The redox peaks detected at 0.33 and 0.16 V in the cyclic voltammetry (CV) plots of the GQDs/Au electrode in Fig. 5 (B) are likely a result of the combined influence of Au and GQDs. Gold nanoparticles are recognized for their superior conductivity and catalytic characteristics, which can improve the electrochemical performance of the electrode. On the other hand, graphene quantum dots, with their unique electronic structure and high surface area, can facilitate electron transfer and provide active sites for electrochemical reactions. The combination of these two materials in the electrode leads to improved redox behavior, as evidenced by the distinct peaks in the CV curves. Incorporating Au nanospheres into the GQDs results in a modified electrochemical response compared to the bare GQDs. The redox peaks at different potentials suggest a synergistic effect between the GQDs and Au, potentially leading to enhanced electrochemical performance. The GQDs/Au composite demonstrates a higher current than GQDs, which can be attributed to its smaller bandgap. Incorporating Au nanoparticles into GQDs introduces intermediate energy levels, effectively reducing the band gap of the composite material. This reduction in bandgap facilitates the movement of electrons, resulting in enhanced electrical conductivity and higher current density.

Furthermore, the influence of scan rate on the CV curves is evident. As the scan rate increases, the shape of the CV curves changes, specifically the decrease in the internal area of the curves. This phenomenon occurs because, at higher scan rates, the time available for the faradaic processes on the electrode's surface becomes shorter. Consequently, a portion of the electrode's surface becomes less accessible, reducing the internal area of the CV curves. The observed trends in current density and the effect of scan rate provide valuable insights into the electrochemical behavior of the GQDs/Au composite. The reduced bandgap and the scan rate-dependent changes in the CV curves highlight the importance of material composition and experimental parameters in optimizing the performance of composite electrodes.

In Fig. 5 (C, D), you can find the galvanostatic charging/discharging (GCD) curves of the samples at different current densities ranging from 1 to 4 A/g. It is worth noting that as the current density increases, the area under the discharge curve tends to decrease. This phenomenon can be attributed to the shorter time required for charge transfer between electrolyte and electrode through faradaic processes. The reduced discharge time indicates a more efficient charge transfer process at higher current densities. Fig. 5 (E) shows the GCD curves of the GQDs and GQDs/Au nanocomposite within the potential window of 0–0.4 V at a current density of 1 A/g. Notably, the GQDs/Au nanocomposite exhibits a longer discharge time compared to bare GQDs, indicating improved charge transfer characteristics between the nanocomposite and the electrolyte. The galvanostatic discharge curve at a current density of 1 A/g was utilized to determine the specific capacitance. The specific capacitance of the samples was estimated using [42]:
(2)
C sp
=
2 I
∫
t i
t f
V
t
dt
m
∆ V
2
where the I/m ratio denotes current density and
∫
t i
t f
V
t
dt signifies the area under the discharge curve. The specific energy and power of the samples were estimated utilizing the following equations [43,44]:
(3)
E = 3.6
1 2
C sp
∆
V 2
(4)
P = 3600
E Δt
where ∆V denotes the potential window (V), Csp denotes the specific capacitance, and t denotes the discharge time (s). Specific power refers to the power per unit mass, while specific energy represents the energy storage capacity per unit mass. The specific capacitance, energy, and power, which are important metrics for evaluating the performance of the samples, are reported in Table 1 .

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis was performed to investigate the resistivity and charge transport characteristics at the interface of electrode/electrolyte, as shown in Fig. 6 (A). The analysis of EIS data is essential for gaining insights into the electrochemical performance and efficiency of the samples under study. EIS plots serve as valuable tools for characterizing the impedance behavior of the electrode-electrolyte system across a range of frequencies. In an ideal supercapacitor system, the Nyquist plot typically displays a straight line with a pronounced increase in impedance at lower frequencies, indicative of capacitive behavior primarily driven by the storage of ionic and electronic charges. The point where this line intersects the horizontal axis is the electronic charge transfer resistance (Rct) and the internal resistance (Rs) associated with the surface electrochemical processes and the overall resistance of the electrode system, respectively. This information aids in understanding the charge transfer processes and overall performance of the supercapacitor device. Fig. 6 (B) illustrates the correlation between specific energy and power, offering valuable insights into the balance between energy storage capacity and power output. This graphical representation facilitates a deeper understanding of the performance attributes of the samples concerning their energy storage capabilities and power delivery requirements. By analyzing this plot, researchers can assess the trade-off relationship between energy and power, which is crucial for optimizing the design and functionality of energy storage devices. Fig. 6 (C) demonstrates the capacity retention of the samples over 2000 charge-discharge cycles. Capacity retention refers to the capability of the electrode to maintain its charge storage capacity over repeated charge-discharge cycling. The GQDs/Au electrode exhibits a specific capacitance retention of approximately ~79 %, indicating its reasonable stability and durability.

A practical application of the GQDs/Au nanocomposite electrode was demonstrated using a 3 M KOH aqueous electrolyte solution in an asymmetric two-electrode setup to act as a supercapacitor electrode. The positive electrode in this configuration was the GQDs/Au working electrode, while the negative electrode was made of activated carbon. The choice of the GQDs/Au nanocomposite as the positive electrode and activated carbon as the negative electrode in the supercapacitor device was based on their complementary characteristics. Activated carbon is known for its high surface area and excellent capacitive behavior, making it an ideal material for efficient energy storage in the negative electrode. By combining these two electrodes in an asymmetric configuration, the fabricated supercapacitor device can balance high energy and power densities, thus enabling efficient energy storage and rapid energy release. Using the 3 M KOH aqueous electrolyte solution ensures effective ion transport and facilitates the electrochemical processes occurring over the surface of the electrodes [45].

First, we evaluated the CV curves of the two-electrode setup by expanding the potential window from 0.5 to 1.5 V, as shown in Fig. 7 (A). The GQDs/Au electrode demonstrates a specific capacitance that is advantageous, implying its capability to retain a considerable quantity of electrical charge within the potential extended range. Fig. 7 (B) illustrates the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves obtained at various scan rates. The presence of distinct faradic peaks arises from the functional groups located on the surface of GQDs, which contribute to redox reactions during the electrochemical processes. The appearance of these peaks indicates the capacitive behavior of the electrode material. To examine the performance of the GQDs/Au electrode under different current densities, galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curves were recorded and presented in Fig. 7 (C). The non-linear behavior observed in these curves can be attributed to the combined contribution of the electric double-layer effect and the pseudocapacitance effect arising from redox reactions associated with oxygen functional groups on the carbon framework. This behavior highlights the energy storage capability of the GQDs/Au electrode. Furthermore, Fig. 7 (D) showcases an image of the assembled asymmetric supercapacitor effectively illuminating a red LED, demonstrating its potential to power small electronic devices. This demonstration emphasizes the potential of the GQDs/Au electrode in providing sustainable and efficient energy storage solutions.

The first-principles density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed with the Quantum Espresso package (V.7.1) [46]. We also utilized norm-conserving pseudopotentials [47]. We also utilized the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with PBE electron exchange-correlation functionals [48] was utilized. For wavefunctions, we set a kinetic energy cutoff of 80 Ry, and the first Brillouin zone k-point mesh was 6 × 6 × 1. Structural optimization involved criteria of 10−6 Ry and 10−3 Ry/Bohr for convergence of energy and force, respectively. To prevent interactions between adjacent cells, we included a large vacuum (~12 Å). We utilized the dipole correction technique to rectify inaccuracies that arose due to periodic boundary conditions [49]. Our initial step was to create an interface between Au (111) and graphene, with a very small lattice mismatch of 0.06 %. We allowed the atoms to relax within the cell, excluding Au atoms not on the exposed surface to graphene. After performing calculations on the electrostatic potential of the Au(111)/Graphene interface perpendicular to it, as shown in Fig. 8 , we noticed a considerable difference of 5.8 eV in electrostatic potentials across the layers. This difference resulted in a substantial internal electric field (Ein) directed from Au toward graphene. Consequently, the internal electric field can facilitate the movement of electrons from graphene toward Au, enhancing the interface's charge storage capacity.

In summary, GQDs and the GQDs/Au nanocomposite were successfully synthesized using hydrothermal and photochemical methods on nickel foam substrates. The GQDs/Au nanocomposite electrode exhibited a 2.5× improved specific capacitance than the pure GQDs electrode. In particular, the nanocomposite demonstrated a high specific capacitance (480 F/g), Additionally, it exhibited specific power and specific energy values of 106 Wh/kg and 10.6 W/kg, respectively, when tested at a current density of 1 A/g. The enhancement is due to the improved charge transfer kinetics due to the incorporation of Au nanoparticles based on DFT results. In addition, the GQDs/Au nanocomposite electrode displayed improved charge-discharge cycling stability than the pure GQDs electrode which is appropriate for its long-term performance. Furthermore, the GQDs/Au nanocomposite was shown to be able to power on a red LED in an asymmetric supercapacitor setup.

Samira Sharifi: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization, Software, Visualization. Mohamad Taghi Dejpasand: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Visualization. Kourosh Rahimi: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Ahmad Yazdani: Conceptualization, Investigation. Esmaiel Saievar-Iranizad: Conceptualization, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical supercapacitors for energy storage and conversion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.K. Kim, S. Sy, A. Yu, J. Zhang, Electrochemical Supercapacitors for Energy Storage and Conversion, in: Handbook of Clean Energy Systems, John Wiley & Sons, Ltd, Chichester, UK, 2015: pp. 1–25. doi:10.1002/9781118991978.hces112.
